metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the word-count range for `docs/your-first-change.md`: pick one upper bound (1200 or 1500) and use it in both the 'What' section and AC1.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 800–1200 words in the 'What' section but 800–1500 words in AC1 — an executor cannot know which upper bound is authoritative without stopping to ask.
findings.location: What section ('800–1200 words') vs AC line 1 ('800–1500 words')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC1 to '800–1200 words' to match the What section, or update the What section to '800–1500 words' to match AC1 — either is fine, just make them agree.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concretely how an external contributor obtains a rebar ticket (e.g. 'open a GitHub Discussion; a maintainer assigns a ticket ID; use that ID in the trailer') so the executor has a specific flow to document.
findings.finding: The plan defers to execution the design question of how an external contributor obtains or references a rebar ticket — 'explain how an outsider gets/references a ticket, coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories' is an open design choice, not a stated approach.
findings.location: What section, tutorial beat 3 ('commit including the `rebar-ticket:` trailer … explain how an outsider gets/references a ticket')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Convert AC5 to a repo-durable artifact: record the demo-asset form decision in a comment inside the plan doc or in an ADR entry (e.g. a line in `docs/decisions.md`) so the choice is verifiable from the repo.
findings.finding: AC5 ('Demo-asset form decision recorded on the ticket') is a one-time external action on a ticket system, not a durable repo artifact — it cannot be verified from the repo, is non-durable (the ticket could be closed/deleted), and is post-session if the ticket system is external.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5 with: '[ ] The chosen demo-asset form (static screenshot / gif / asciinema) is recorded in `docs/decisions.md` (or an inline comment in README.md) with a one-sentence rationale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make AC2 concrete by enumerating the specific internal-knowledge markers that must be absent (e.g. 'no mention of dual-remote layout, no driver-group names, no Nava-internal Jira project keys, no VPN requirement') so it can be checked as a finite list rather than a subjective judgment.
findings.finding: AC2 ('external happy path requires zero Nava-internal knowledge') is a subjective, unobservable criterion with no concrete test — 'zero internal knowledge' cannot be evaluated mechanically and relies on reviewer judgment about what counts as internal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC2 with: '[ ] `docs/your-first-change.md` contains none of the following: dual-remote checkout instructions, driver-group names, any Nava-internal system name other than rebar.solutions.navateam.com and the Gerrit instance URL.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the word-count range for `docs/your-first-change.md`: the What section says 800–1200 words but AC1 says 800–1500 — pick one upper bound and apply it consistently.
findings.finding: The plan body specifies a word count of '800–1200 words' for `docs/your-first-change.md`, but AC1 specifies '800–1500 words'. These are contradictory upper bounds that will leave the implementer uncertain whether 1300 words passes or fails verification.
findings.location: What section (first bullet) vs Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt 800–1500 in both places (the wider range matches AC1 and gives the author room for the gotchas table without cutting content).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-audit what the CONTRIBUTING.md restructure actually needs to change: the current file already leads with the external contributor path (§1–§2 use `origin`=Gerrit, no dual-remote), so the plan should specify *what new content* is missing (e.g., the tutorial cross-link, the shepherded-patch/DCO stubs) rather than asserting a structural inversion that does not reflect current reality.
findings.finding: The plan's core restructure rationale asserts 'CONTRIBUTING.md assumes Nava-internal context (the dual-remote checkout layout, feature-branch driver groups)' and that 'there is no from-zero path for an external contributor,' but reading CONTRIBUTING.md shows the opposite: it uses only `origin` (no dual-remote at all), §1 already provides a complete external path (GitHub OAuth → HTTP password → clone from Gerrit → commit-msg hook), and §4 already places feature-branch mechanics after the main loop with a clear 'when to use this' caveat. The dual-remote layout is in CLAUDE.md (line 498), not CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.location: Why section; CONTRIBUTING.md restructure bullet in What section; AC item 3 ('CONTRIBUTING.md leads with the external path')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Why rationale's claim about dual-remote in CONTRIBUTING.md with an accurate diagnosis: 'CONTRIBUTING.md's external path exists but lacks a tutorial cross-link and does not explain how an outsider gets a ticket or deals with the Contributors-group submit restriction; CLAUDE.md (not CONTRIBUTING.md) holds the Nava-internal dual-remote note.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the demo-asset form decision (screenshot vs gif vs asciinema) explicitly in the plan before implementation — or split AC4 into two criteria: one for 'decision recorded' and one for 'asset present at ~line 40' — so the AC is verifiable rather than contingent on an outstanding open question.
findings.finding: The plan prescribes a README front-door pass adding 'an image by ~line 40' as a convergent pattern, but no demo image or asset currently exists anywhere in the repository (no image files in docs/, no screenshot or gif referenced in README.md). The plan lists the demo-asset form as an 'open decision,' which means this deliverable is unresolved at plan-writing time — yet AC4 requires 'image by ~line 40' as a passing criterion.
findings.location: What section — README front-door pass bullet; AC item 4 ('README top matches the pattern: tagline, ≤6 badges, image by ~line 40'); Open decision paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism by which an outsider obtains a valid rebar ticket ID before writing their first commit — e.g., 'maintainer creates a ticket and shares the alias' or 'outsider creates a ticket via rebar CLI after signing in' — so the tutorial's rebar-ticket trailer beat can give actionable instructions without waiting for sibling stories.
findings.finding: The `rebar-ticket:` trailer beat is mechanically under-specified for outsiders: the plan acknowledges this is 'a rebar-specific beat no exemplar has' and says to 'explain how an outsider gets/references a ticket,' but defers the solution to coordination with sibling stories. The CI Verified gate enforces this trailer by resolving the ID against the live ticket store — the outsider must have a real ticket ID in that store, not a GitHub issue number. Neither path (outsider self-creates a ticket, or maintainer creates one) is specified in this plan, leaving the tutorial unable to give actionable instructions for this mandatory step.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('commit including the rebar-ticket: trailer … explain how an outsider gets/references a ticket, coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories'); AC1 (nine-beat sequence includes 'commit w/ trailer')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `indeterminate` to the coverage-gap sub-reason list in the tutorial's 'reading a −1' beat and gotchas table, matching all five values in `_TAG_SUFFIXES` (`gate-disabled`, `llm-unavailable`, `scanner`, `review-error`, `indeterminate`).
findings.finding: The plan's tutorial specifies the `coverage-gap` sub-reason vocabulary as 'llm-unavailable / scanner / gate-disabled / review-error' (four values), but the authoritative `_TAG_SUFFIXES` enum in `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` defines five sub-reasons — the plan omits `indeterminate`. A contributor who receives `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap (indeterminate)]` will find no matching entry in the tutorial's 'reading a −1' table or gotchas table, leaving them without guidance on this infra-veto variant.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('reading a −1 … BLOCK — coverage-gap infra veto'); AC1 ('both bot votes and the gotchas table'); src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py lines 44–52
findings.suggested_fix: In the gotchas table and the 'reading a −1' prose, replace the four-item list with: `BLOCK — coverage-gap (gate-disabled | llm-unavailable | scanner | review-error | indeterminate)` — all five sub-reasons from `_TAG_SUFFIXES`. Add a note that any `coverage-gap` tag is an infra veto; the sub-reason identifies which leg failed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an in-scope, self-contained fallback for the `rebar-ticket:` beat: either specify the exact step an outsider takes to obtain/request a ticket (e.g., open a GitHub Discussion, email a maintainer, or use a designated 'first-contribution' ticket alias), or add a gotchas-table entry for 'no ticket assigned yet' so the tutorial does not depend on sibling stories to be complete.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the tutorial must instruct outsiders to include a `rebar-ticket:` trailer in their commit (beat 3), but defers the question of how an outsider obtains a ticket to 'sibling stories' with no in-scope fallback or placeholder. If those sibling stories are incomplete or arrive after this tutorial, an outsider following the tutorial step-by-step hits a mandatory gate step with no resolution path — the tutorial's own gotchas table and 'See-also' section are silent on this state.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('commit including the `rebar-ticket:` trailer … coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories'); AC1 (nine-beat sequence including 'commit w/ trailer'); AC2 ('zero Nava-internal knowledge')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the word-count target to a single range across the What section and AC1 (recommend 800–1500 as the more generous bound given the nine-beat sequence plus gotchas table).
findings.finding: The word-count target for `docs/your-first-change.md` is stated as 800–1200 words in the What section but 800–1500 words in AC1 — an internal inconsistency that gives the implementer ambiguous acceptance bounds.
findings.location: What section ('800–1200 words'); AC1 ('800–1500 words')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the What section to read '800–1500 words' to match AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the push command in the tutorial from `git push gerrit HEAD:refs/for/main` to `git push origin HEAD:refs/for/main`, consistent with the outsider clone-from-Gerrit convention established in CONTRIBUTING.md and confirmed by CLAUDE.md.
findings.finding: The plan's tutorial pushes code with 'git push gerrit HEAD:refs/for/main' (naming the remote 'gerrit'), but the established outsider path — cloning from Gerrit so 'origin' IS Gerrit — uses 'git push origin HEAD:refs/for/main'. CONTRIBUTING.md and CLAUDE.md both confirm that external contributors use 'origin' for the Gerrit push. Using 'gerrit' as the remote name in the tutorial will require outsiders to add a second remote or will produce a 'no such remote' error, breaking the zero-internal-knowledge AC.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('git push gerrit HEAD:refs/for/main'); AC line 2 ('zero Nava-internal knowledge')
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git push origin HEAD:refs/for/main` throughout `docs/your-first-change.md` (matching CONTRIBUTING.md §2b). Optionally add one sentence noting that internal contributors name this remote 'gerrit' instead — directing dual-remote users to CONTRIBUTING.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an in-scope fallback to the `rebar-ticket:` beat: either specify what an outsider does when they have no ticket (e.g., open a GitHub Discussion, reference a placeholder, or contact a maintainer), or add a gotchas-table entry for 'no ticket assigned' so the tutorial is self-contained regardless of sibling-story status.
findings.finding: The tutorial requires an outsider to include a `rebar-ticket:` trailer in their commit, but the plan defers 'how an outsider gets/references a ticket' entirely to sibling stories with no in-scope fallback. If those sibling stories are incomplete or the outsider arrives before they land, the tutorial leaves them stuck at a mandatory step with no guidance — an unresolved non-happy-path state for the most workflow-critical beat.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('commit including the `rebar-ticket:` trailer … explain how an outsider gets/references a ticket, coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories'); AC1 (nine-beat sequence)
findings.suggested_fix: In beat 3, add one sentence: 'If you don't yet have a ticket, open a GitHub Discussion in this repo to request one before pushing — reviewers will decline changes that lack a tracker reference.' Add a corresponding row to the gotchas table: 'No rebar-ticket trailer → Open a GitHub Discussion to request a ticket; do not push without one.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'cross-link the shepherded-patch and DCO sections owned by sibling stories' directive with either (a) a placeholder strategy (e.g. TODO comments until sibling stories deliver) or (b) a guard condition on sequencing — and explicitly name the sibling ticket IDs so delivery dependency is trackable.
findings.finding: The plan directs CONTRIBUTING.md to 'cross-link the shepherded-patch and DCO sections owned by sibling stories,' but no file in the repo contains a `shepherded-patch` or `DCO` section — these are forward references to content that does not yet exist and whose delivery is not guaranteed by this ticket.
findings.location: What section, CONTRIBUTING.md restructure bullet / CONTRIBUTING.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a beat to the tutorial (between re-push and submit) that explains the Contributors group authorization requirement — outsiders can propose a change freely but landing it requires an admin to grant Submit access; specify the ask-an-admin path, or that a maintainer can submit on their behalf.
findings.finding: The tutorial's 'submit' step (beat 3) will silently fail for any outsider not already in the Gerrit `Contributors` group. The existing CONTRIBUTING.md §2e has a callout box for this ('Submitting requires contributor authorization … ask an admin'), but the plan's tutorial beat sequence ends with '→ submit' without specifying that this step requires prior maintainer authorization. An outsider who reaches this point will see a greyed-out Submit button with no explanation in the tutorial, breaking the 'zero Nava-internal knowledge' promise of AC2.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('Your first change … → submit'); AC2 ('The external happy path requires zero Nava-internal knowledge'); infra/gerrit/project.config line 53 (submit = group Contributors); CONTRIBUTING.md §2e
findings.suggested_fix: In beat 3, after the re-push step and before 'submit,' add a brief note: 'Note: the Submit button is restricted to authorized contributors. If it isn't available to you, ask a maintainer to submit on your behalf or to add you to the Contributors group — see CONTRIBUTING.md for the full procedure.' This keeps the tutorial accurate without requiring the outsider to know the Gerrit group model upfront.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit beat (or gotcha-table row) in the tutorial explaining the Contributors group Submit restriction: note that an outsider with both votes at +1 must ask a maintainer to submit (or be added to Contributors), and describe how to signal readiness — do not leave beat 9 implying self-service Submit is available.
findings.finding: The tutorial's AC beat 9 ('submit') and the tutorial narrative ('→ submit') assert an outsider completes the full end-to-end loop including the Submit action, but the live Gerrit Submit ACL (project.config) restricts Submit to the 'Contributors' group + Administrators — any GitHub-OAuth user may PROPOSE a change but cannot LAND it without explicit Contributors group membership. The plan defers resolution entirely to sibling stories ('coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories') with no fallback or placeholder for the case where those stories aren't yet shipped.
findings.location: What section beat 3 ('→ submit'); AC line 1 ('nine-beat convergent sequence … submit')
findings.suggested_fix: In the tutorial's 'submit' beat, add: 'Once both votes are +1 and no comments are unresolved, the Submit button appears — but Submit requires Contributors group membership. If it is grayed out, post a comment on your change asking a maintainer to submit it (or to add you to the Contributors group). This is by design: anyone may propose a change; landing it requires explicit authorization.' Also add a gotcha-table row: 'Submit button missing / greyed out → You are not in the Contributors group; ask a maintainer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC — e.g. `wc -w docs/your-first-change.md` for AC1's word count, `grep -n 'your-first-change' CONTRIBUTING.md | head -5` to confirm AC3's placement, and `awk '/```/,/```/' README.md | wc -l` for AC4's quickstart length.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command — for documentation deliverables, at minimum a `wc -w` for word count, a `grep` for the tutorial link in CONTRIBUTING.md, and a `wc -l` or `awk` for the ≤15-line quickstart would make each AC mechanically verifiable rather than inspection-only.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all five ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting the README front-door pass (tagline, badges, demo asset, quickstart) into its own ticket/PR, keeping the tutorial + CONTRIBUTING restructure as one coupled unit; verify that neither piece is worthless alone before splitting.
findings.finding: The ticket bundles two independently-valuable outcomes with distinct primary actors/personas: (1) contributor-onboarding docs (tutorial + CONTRIBUTING restructure, actor = prospective external contributor) and (2) README front-door pass (actor = prospective user/evaluator discovering the project). The README work — tagline, badges, demo asset, quickstart — delivers user-facing discoverability value regardless of whether a contribution tutorial exists, and the contribution tutorial delivers zero incremental value to that actor. Each piece is independently reviewable, mergeable, and releasable. The open 'demo-asset form' decision embedded in the README work could block or delay the contributor onboarding piece unnecessarily.
findings.location: ## What (third bullet: README front-door pass) and AC line 4: 'README top matches the pattern: tagline, ≤6 badges, image by ~line 40, all-three-surfaces quickstart in ≤15 lines.'
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two tickets: (A) contributor-onboarding: docs/your-first-change.md + CONTRIBUTING.md restructure (tightly coupled, keep together); (B) README front-door: tagline, badges, demo asset, quickstart (independent user-facing concern). Resolve the demo-asset open decision in ticket B without blocking ticket A.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the sibling stories by ticket ID (or working file path) for the 'shepherded-patch' and 'DCO' sections, and add them as explicit dependency edges in the plan.
findings.finding: The sibling stories referenced as 'shepherded-patch' and 'DCO stories' are never identified by name, ticket ID, or file path, leaving the executor unable to locate the sections to cross-link without investigation.
findings.location: What section — CONTRIBUTING.md restructure bullet; tutorial beat 3 ('rebar-ticket: trailer … coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Dependencies section listing e.g. 'Depends on: [TICKET-X] DCO sign-off story (for docs/dco.md section anchor) and [TICKET-Y] shepherded-patch story (for docs/shepherded-patch.md section anchor)'; if the sections don't exist yet, state the placeholder anchor text to use.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the badge specification: the What section enumerates exactly 4 badges but AC4 allows ≤6 — either update AC4 to '= 4 badges (PyPI version, Python versions, CI, license)' or update the What section to say '4–6 badges'.
findings.finding: The plan body specifies exactly '4 badges' for the README, but AC4 specifies '≤6 badges'. An implementer following the body would add exactly 4; one following the AC could add up to 6 — these are contradictory specifications.
findings.location: What section (README front-door pass bullet) vs Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC4 to read '4 badges (PyPI version, Python versions, CI, license)' to match the explicit enumeration in the What section, since the four named badges are already intentional choices.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit merge-order constraint (or placeholder-link strategy) for the CONTRIBUTING.md cross-links to the shepherded-patch and DCO sections, so the tutorial does not ship with dangling references if sibling stories are unmerged.
findings.finding: The plan directs CONTRIBUTING.md to cross-link 'the shepherded-patch and DCO sections owned by sibling stories,' but provides no sequencing guard or placeholder strategy for the case where those sibling stories are not merged before this one. The cross-links will be dangling references at merge time if the sibling stories land later.
findings.location: What section — CONTRIBUTING.md restructure bullet; AC line 3 ('cross-link the shepherded-patch and DCO sections owned by sibling stories')
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) note a merge-order dependency ('this story merges after the DCO and shepherded-patch stories'), or (b) specify that cross-links are written as plain text TODOs/stub headings until those sections exist, to be replaced in a follow-up commit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the explicit dependency on the shepherded-patch and DCO sibling stories (link the ticket IDs) and specify a fallback strategy if those stories haven't landed yet — e.g., 'if sibling sections are absent, use a placeholder note directing readers to ask on the ticket' — so the implementer has a clear path regardless of story delivery order.
findings.finding: The plan directs CONTRIBUTING.md to 'cross-link the shepherded-patch and DCO sections owned by sibling stories' and the tutorial's beat 3 to coordinate with those same sibling stories, but provides no sequencing guard or placeholder strategy for the case where those sibling stories haven't shipped yet. If this story lands first, the cross-links will dangle or the tutorial will have an unexplained gap.
findings.location: What section, CONTRIBUTING.md bullet ('cross-link the shepherded-patch and DCO sections owned by sibling stories'); beat 3 ('coordinated with the shepherded-patch and DCO stories')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name at least two of the six 'convergent dev-tool pattern' prior-art projects and confirm at least one is a multi-surface system (CLI + library or CLI + SDK), or cite an alternative multi-surface README pattern if the dev-tool set proves to be single-surface-only.
findings.finding: The README front-door restructure is justified by 'the 6/6 convergent dev-tool pattern (ruff/uv/httpx/rich/just/fzf)' — six named CLI tools that are each single-purpose — but rebar is a multi-surface system (CLI + Python library + MCP server + Jira reconciler). The plan asserts this pattern is universal ('convergent') without naming the six surveyed projects or verifying that any multi-surface/SDK product in that set matches rebar's shape. An executor spec'ing '≤6 badges', 'image by ~line 40', and a '≤15-line quickstart covering all three surfaces' would be implementing a pattern derived from analogues that may not transfer.
findings.location: Why section / README front-door pass paragraph; AC line 4 ('README top matches the pattern: tagline, ≤6 badges, image by ~line 40, all-three-surfaces quickstart in ≤15 lines')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical naming 2–3 of the 6 surveyed projects (e.g. '(ruff, uv, httpx — all single-surface CLI tools)') and either (a) confirm that multi-surface tools in the same ecosystem (e.g. httpx which has both sync/async APIs) use the same pattern, or (b) note a multi-surface exception and document how the ≤15-line three-surface quickstart addresses it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a gotchas-table row (or a 'Troubleshooting' callout box) covering at least: OAuth sign-in failure (link to where to ask for access), hook installation verification (`git log --format=%B -n1 | grep Change-Id`), and push authentication error (confirm HTTP password is saved in git credential store).
findings.finding: The gotchas table and setup beats omit account-setup failure states (OAuth sign-in failure, HTTP password generation errors, commit-msg hook installation failure) — the most common abandonment points for a first-time external contributor who has never used this Gerrit instance.
findings.location: What section, beat 2 (one-time setup); beat 4 (gotchas table)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the gotchas table with: 'OAuth sign-in fails → email maintainers@… or open a GitHub Discussion; Gerrit access is not automatic.' and 'Hook seems installed but Change-Id is still missing → run `git log --format=%B -n1 | grep Change-Id` to verify; re-run the curl command if empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the Scope IN list and include a step to register `docs/your-first-change.md` in the Contributor section of the docs index.
findings.finding: `docs/README.md` (the audience-indexed docs table of contents) is an unenumerated consumer of the new `docs/your-first-change.md` file: its Contributor section does not list the tutorial and is not named as an edit target in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: Scope section / docs/README.md (Contributor section, lines 58–90)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope IN: `docs/README.md` (Contributor section — add entry for `your-first-change.md`). In the What section, add: '`docs/README.md` Contributor section: prepend an entry for the new tutorial, one line, linking and describing it as the entry point for new external contributors.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `CLAUDE.md` (contribution section) to the Scope IN list as a secondary edit target, or explicitly note it as an OUT-of-scope consumer requiring a follow-up update if CONTRIBUTING.md section numbering changes.
findings.finding: `CLAUDE.md` is an unenumerated consumer of CONTRIBUTING.md whose contribution section (lines 486–556) mirrors CONTRIBUTING.md's external vs. dual-remote structure and explicitly notes 'external contributors use CONTRIBUTING.md's origin commands verbatim.' The CONTRIBUTING.md restructure (external path first, internal mechanics moved after) may render CLAUDE.md's contribution section inconsistent without it being named as an edit target.
findings.location: What section, CONTRIBUTING.md restructure bullet / CLAUDE.md lines 486–556

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide explicitly whether `.github/pull_request_template.md` should link to `docs/your-first-change.md` directly (as the front-door target) and add it to Scope IN or document it as a conscious OUT.
findings.finding: `.github/pull_request_template.md` is an unenumerated consumer that currently serves as an outsider's first contact point when they open a GitHub PR — it links to CONTRIBUTING.md but not to the new `docs/your-first-change.md` tutorial. It is not named as an edit target.
findings.location: Scope section / .github/pull_request_template.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either drop the 'JGit proves…' justification (the tutorial is obviously warranted on its own merits) or replace it with a Gerrit project that uses GitHub OAuth as its auth provider (e.g. GerritHub-hosted projects) to make the analogy technically sound.
findings.finding: The plan states 'JGit proves a GitHub-OAuth Gerrit supports a very short one (~250 words)' to justify tutorial length/scope, but JGit's Gerrit uses Eclipse Foundation OAuth (or LDAP/OpenID), not GitHub OAuth, and JGit's Gerrit is hosted on a well-known public instance (gerrit.googlesource.com / eclipse.gerrithub.io) with different clone URL structure than rebar's self-hosted AWS instance. The analogy is used as proof-of-concept for the GitHub-OAuth-specific setup steps (HTTP password from Settings → HTTP Credentials, the /a/ prefix clone URL), which JGit's tutorial would not cover.
findings.location: Why section: 'JGit proves a GitHub-OAuth Gerrit supports a very short one (~250 words)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
